extendedContent.content:
The Ring of Gyges appears in Plato's Republic as a thought experiment about justice and
                    morality. According to the tale told by Glaucon, Gyges was a shepherd in the service of
                    the King of Lydia. One day, after a great storm and earthquake, a chasm opened in the
                    earth near where he was feeding his flock.
                
                
                    Curious, Gyges descended into the chasm and discovered, among other wonders, a hollow
                    bronze horse with doors. Inside was the corpse of a being larger than a man, wearing
                    nothing but a golden ring on its finger. Gyges took the ring and returned to the surface.
                
                
                    Later, while sitting among other shepherds, Gyges happened to turn the bezel of the ring
                    inward toward his palm. Instantly, he became invisible to his companions, who spoke of
                    him as if he had left. Turning the bezel outward again made him visible. Once he
                    understood the ring's power, Gyges arranged to be one of the messengers sent to the king.
                    There he seduced the queen, killed the king with her help, and seized the throne.
extendedContent.title: The Story of Gyges

extendedContent.content:
The Question of Justice
                
                    Glaucon presents this story to challenge Socrates: if anyone possessed such a ring and
                    could act with complete impunity, would they remain just? The implication is that people
                    behave justly only because they fear the consequences of injustice. Remove the fear of
                    being caught, and even the most "just" person would act unjustly.
                

                
                    "Suppose now that there were two such magic rings, and the just put on one of them and
                    the unjust the other; no man can be imagined to be of such an iron nature that he would
                    stand fast in justice... this, many would say, is a great proof that a man is just, not
                    willingly or because he thinks that justice is any good to him individually, but of
                    necessity, for wherever anyone thinks that he can safely be unjust, there he is unjust."
                    — Plato, Republic, Book II
                

                Socrates' Response
                
                    The rest of the Republic can be seen as Socrates' extended response to this challenge.
                    He argues that justice is intrinsically valuable, that the just person is happier than
                    the unjust regardless of external consequences, and that a healthy soul requires
                    justice as a healthy body requires proper function of its parts.
extendedContent.title: Philosophical Significance

extendedContent.content:
The Ring of Gyges has profoundly influenced Western thought about ethics, power, and
                    human nature. It raised questions that remain central to moral philosophy: Is morality
                    merely conventional? Do we act rightly only from fear of punishment? What would we do
                    with unlimited power and no accountability?
                
                
                    The ring also became the prototype for magical rings of invisibility in later literature,
                    most famously influencing J.R.R. Tolkien's One Ring, which similarly tests its bearers'
                    moral character and corrupts those who use it.
extendedContent.title: Cultural Legacy

name: Ring of Gyges

cultural.modernLegacy: The Ring of Gyges continues to be a relevant philosophical concept, appearing in literature, film, and ethical discussions. It serves as a cautionary tale about the dangers of unchecked power and the importance of moral character. Its central question—would people be moral if they could act without consequence?—remains a subject of debate in contemporary society.

shortDescription: The Philosopher's Ring of Invisibility and Moral Question
longDescription:
The Philosopher's Ring of Invisibility and Moral Question The Ring of Gyges appears in Plato's Republic as a thought experiment about justice and
                    morality. According to the tale told by Glaucon, Gyges was a shepherd in the service of
                    the King of Lydia. One day, after a great storm and earthquake, a chasm opened in the
                    earth near where he was feeding his flock.
symbolism:
The Question of Justice
                
                    Glaucon presents this story to challenge Socrates: if anyone possessed such a ring and
                    could act with complete impunity, would they remain just? The implication is that people
                    behave justly only because they fear the consequences of injustice. Remove the fear of
                    being caught, and even the most "just" person would act unjustly.
                

                
                    "Suppose now that there were two such magic rings, and the just put on one of them and
                    the unjust the other; no man can be imagined to be of such an iron nature that he would
                    stand fast in justice... this, many would say, is a great proof that a man is just, not
                    willingly or because he thinks that justice is any good to him individually, but of
                    necessity, for wherever anyone thinks that he can safely be unjust, there he is unjust."
                    — Plato, Republic, Book II
                

                Socrates' Response
                
                    The rest of the Republic can be seen as Socrates' extended response to this challenge.
                    He argues that justice is intrinsically valuable, that the just person is happier than
                    the unjust regardless of external consequences, and that a healthy soul requires
                    justice as a healthy body requires proper function of its parts.
description: The Ring of Gyges is a mythical artifact most famously discussed in Plato's *Republic*. It is presented as a thought experiment exploring the nature of morality and justice. The story, narrated by Glaucon, tells of a shepherd named Gyges who discovers a golden ring. This ring possesses the power to render its wearer invisible. With this newfound ability, Gyges seduces the queen, murders the king, and seizes the throne. Glaucon uses this tale to argue that people are only just because they fear the consequences of being caught acting unjustly. If given the power to act without consequence, as the ring allows, they would inevitably pursue their own self-interest, regardless of moral considerations. The Ring of Gyges, therefore, serves as a philosophical device to question whether morality is intrinsic or merely a social construct enforced by the fear of punishment. It prompts reflection on human nature and the motivations behind ethical behavior, questioning whether individuals would remain virtuous if all external constraints were removed. The ring embodies the allure of unchecked power and the potential for corruption it represents, challenging the very foundations of moral philosophy.

keyMyths.title: Plato's Republic: The Origin of the Ring
keyMyths.description: The myth of Gyges is first introduced in Plato's Republic (Book II) by Glaucon. He uses the story to challenge Socrates' view that justice is intrinsically valuable, arguing that people only act justly out of fear of punishment and social consequences.

keyMyths.title: The Shepherd Gyges' Discovery
keyMyths.description: Gyges, a shepherd in the service of the King of Lydia, discovers a golden ring after an earthquake creates an opening in the earth. He finds that the ring grants him invisibility when he turns its bezel inward.

keyMyths.title: Gyges' Seduction and Regicide
keyMyths.description: Using the ring's power of invisibility, Gyges seduces the Queen, conspires with her, and assassinates the King. He then seizes the throne of Lydia, becoming the new ruler.

keyMyths.title: The Moral Implications of Invisibility
keyMyths.description: The story serves as a thought experiment, prompting readers to consider whether anyone would consistently act justly if they could be certain of avoiding all consequences for their actions. It probes the nature of morality and self-interest.

keyMyths.title: Variations in Later Interpretations
keyMyths.description: Later interpretations of the myth explore different aspects of power, corruption, and the nature of justice. Some focus on the psychological impact of unchecked power and the temptation it presents.
keyMyths.source: Various philosophical essays and commentaries on Plato's Republic

associations.name: Invisibility
associations.significance: Represents the ability to act without being seen or held accountable.

associations.name: Immorality
associations.significance: Associated with the temptation to act unjustly when consequences are absent.

associations.name: Justice
associations.significance: Raises questions about whether justice is intrinsic or merely a social construct.

associations.name: Corruption
associations.significance: Symbolizes the corrupting influence of unchecked power.

associations.name: Self-interest
associations.significance: Represents the human tendency to prioritize personal gain over moral principles.

associations.name: Ethical dilemma
associations.significance: Poses a fundamental question about human nature and moral motivation.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: A cloak or hood that grants invisibility, often used for trickery or theft.
Recurring motif in folklore, representing hidden action and moral ambiguity.
A helmet granting invisibility, used by Hades to observe without being seen.

subtitle: A Ring Granting Invisibility and Moral Dilemmas

wielders.name: Gyges
wielders.context: Shepherd who discovered and used the ring to usurp the throne.
